content.body: A derivative measures how fast a function changes. An integral does the opposite kind of job: it adds up a function over an interval. Geometrically, that total is the area between the curve and the x-axis.
We write the integral of f from a to b like this — read it as "the area under f from a to b":
Let's build the idea up from the simplest shapes, just like we did with derivatives.
First, get a feel for it: drag x to the right and watch the shaded region — the area between the curve and the x-axis — fill in and grow. That accumulating area is exactly what an integral measures.
Start with the flat line f(x) = 2. Drag x to the right and watch the shaded region grow. It's always a rectangle of height 2. Each time x increases by 1, how much does the shaded area increase?

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit under this different flat line and watch the shaded rectangle's area climb by the same amount each step.

solution.body: Each step to the right adds a thin strip under the flat line $f(x) = 2$. That strip is a rectangle 1 wide and 2 tall:
So the shaded area grows by 2 each step — the option "2".

phases.text: Each step right adds a strip under the flat line — a rectangle $1$ wide and $2$ tall.
Watch the area climb: every unit step adds $1 \times 2 = 2$.
So the shaded area grows by $2$ each step.

feedback.correct: Exactly! The strip you add each step is a 1-by-2 rectangle, so the area grows by 2 every time x increases by 1. Area = height × width.
feedback.incorrect: Look at the new sliver added each step — it's a thin rectangle. How wide is it, and how tall? Its area is width × height.
feedback.hint: Each step adds a rectangle 1 wide and 2 tall — its area is width × height, so multiply those.

content.body: Now tilt the line so f(x) = x, then drag x out to 4. The shaded triangle's area is exactly this integral — evaluate it using area = ½ · base · height.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the shaded triangle's area grow under this different line.

solution.body: The region under $f(x) = x$ from 0 to 4 is a right triangle with base 4 and height $f(4) = 4$.

phases.text: The region under $f(x) = x$ from $0$ to $4$ is a right triangle.
Its base is $4$ and its height is $f(4) = 4$.

feedback.correct: Right! That integral is the area of the triangle — base 4 and height f(4) = 4 — so it equals ½ · 4 · 4 = 8.
feedback.incorrect: The region is a right triangle. Its base runs from 0 to 4, and its height is f(4) = 4.
feedback.hint: Area of a triangle = ½ · base · height — read the base and height off the triangle, then multiply.

content.body: A genuine curve like f(x) = x² isn't a rectangle or a triangle, so there's no single area formula to read off. Instead we slice the region into many thin rectangles, add up their areas, and use that as an estimate.
Drag to add rectangles under f(x) = x² on [0, 2] and watch the running estimate. A few rectangles is rough, but as you add more they hug the curve and the estimate closes in on the true area ≈ 2.67.
You just watched the estimate sharpen as you piled on more rectangles. As the number of rectangles grows without bound — each one infinitely thin — what does the running estimate approach?

options: Zero, since each rectangle becomes infinitely thin
A fixed overestimate that never quite reaches the area
The exact area under the curve — the value of the integral
The height of the curve at the right endpoint

sandbox.caption: Pile more rectangles under this different curve and watch how the running estimate behaves as the strips get thinner.

solution.body: As you add rectangles they hug the curve more tightly and the leftover gaps shrink.
In the limit of infinitely many, infinitely thin rectangles the gaps vanish and the running total reaches the exact area under the curve — the value of the definite integral (the third option).

feedback.correct: Exactly — as the rectangles get thinner and more numerous, the gaps vanish and their total area closes in on the exact area. That limit is the definite integral.
feedback.incorrect: Think about what you just watched: more rectangles hugged the curve more tightly and the estimate settled down rather than blowing up or vanishing.
feedback.hint: As the rectangles get thinner the gaps above them shrink toward nothing, so the total doesn't vanish or stall at an overestimate — it settles on one exact value.

content.body: So a flat line gives a rectangle and a tilted line gives a triangle — shapes with tidy area formulas. A genuine curve has no such formula, but you just saw the workaround: slice the region into many thin rectangles and add them up, and the more rectangles you use, the closer that running total creeps to the true area.
Rectangle, triangle, or a stack of thin slices — either way the integral from a to b means the same thing: the area trapped between the curve and the x-axis over that interval.

content.latex: \int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \text{area under } f \text{ from } a \text{ to } b

content.body: Your turn. The shaded region is the area under f(x) = x from 0 up to t, and the readout below the graph shows the integral building as you drag. Find the value of t that makes this integral equal 2, then drag the slider there.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the shaded area grow as a running total under this different line.

solution.body: The area under $f(x) = x$ from 0 to t is a triangle with base t and height t, so it equals $\tfrac{1}{2}t^2$. Set that equal to 2:

phases.text: The area under $f(x) = x$ from $0$ to $t$ is a triangle: $\frac{1}{2}t^2$.
Drag to $t = 2$, where $2$ units of area have accumulated.

feedback.correct: Nice — the area under f(x) = x from 0 to t is ½ · t², and ½ · 2² = 2, so t = 2. That's where exactly 2 units of area have accumulated.
feedback.incorrect: The area from 0 to t is ½ · t². Set ½ · t² = 2, so t² = 4. Solve for t, then drag the slider there.
feedback.hint: The area from 0 to t is ½ · t² — set that equal to 2 and solve for t.

content.body: Which expression stands for the area under the curve f(x) from x = a to x = b?

options: The integral of f(x) from a to b
The derivative of f(x) at a

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the shaded region accumulate as a live integral under this different curve.

solution.body: Area is an accumulation of the function across the interval, not a slope or a single height.
Slopes are derivatives, and $f(b)$ is just one height. The accumulated area from $a$ to $b$ is the definite integral of $f$ — the first option.

phases.text: Area is an accumulation of $f$ across the interval — not a slope or a single height.
Slopes are derivatives, and $f(b)$ is one height. The accumulated area from $a$ to $b$ is the definite integral.

feedback.correct: Exactly — the definite integral from a to b is precisely the accumulated area under f over that interval.
feedback.incorrect: Area is an accumulation, not a slope or a single height. Which choice describes adding f up across the whole interval?
feedback.hint: Slopes are derivatives, and a single height is just f(b) — so pick the choice that adds f up across the whole interval.

content.body: One important subtlety before we wrap up. Every curve so far has stayed above the x-axis, where each new strip adds a positive amount and the shaded area only grows. But a curve can dip below the axis — and there the strips hang underneath it, so their area counts as NEGATIVE and is subtracted from the running total. This signed total is what the integral really tracks.

See it for yourself. Here f(x) = x − 3 starts below the axis and only climbs above it at x = 3. Drag x to the right and watch the running integral below the graph: it falls while the curve is under the axis, reaches its most negative right where the curve crosses zero, then climbs back as positive area piles on top.

To recap: an integral accumulates a function, and that accumulation is the signed area between the curve and the x-axis — area above the axis adds to the total, while area below it subtracts. Flat lines give rectangles, tilted lines give triangles, and for a genuine curve you slice the region into many thin rectangles and add them up — the more slices, the sharper the estimate.
Next up: we'll let those slices get infinitely thin so the estimate becomes exact, then find a shortcut that hands you the area without summing rectangles by hand.
The shaded region is the area under f(x) = x from 0 to t. Drag t until that area is 8.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the shaded area build up under this different line.

feedback.correct: Nice — ½ · 4² = 8, so t = 4 gives exactly 8 units of area.
feedback.incorrect: Set ½t² = 8, so t² = 16. Solve for t, then drag the slider there.
feedback.hint: The area from 0 to t is ½t² — set ½t² = 8 and solve for t.

content.body: For the flat line f(x) = 2, drag t until the shaded area from 0 to t is 6.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit under this different flat line and watch the rectangle's area build up.

feedback.correct: Right — 2 · 3 = 6, so t = 3 encloses 6 units of area.
feedback.incorrect: The area is height × width = 2t. Set 2t = 6 and solve.
feedback.hint: The rectangle's area is height × width = 2t — set 2t = 6 and solve for t.

content.body: This time the shaded region starts at x = 1, not 0. Drag t until the area under f(x) = x from 1 to t equals 4.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the shaded area pile up under this different line.

feedback.correct: Nice — (t² − 1)/2 = 4 gives t² = 9, so t = 3. Starting at 1 instead of 0 leaves out the little triangle below x = 1.
feedback.incorrect: Set (t² − 1)/2 = 4, so t² − 1 = 8 and t² = 9. Solve for t, then drag the slider there.
feedback.hint: The area from 1 to t is (t² − 1)/2 — set it equal to 4 and solve for t.

content.body: Match each integral to its value. Watch out: where the curve dips below the x-axis the area is negative, so some values are negative or zero.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on this different line and watch the signed area — the stretch below the axis subtracts.

feedback.correct: Exactly — ∫₀² x = 2 and ∫₁³ x = 4 are positive areas, ∫₀² (x−2) = −2 sits below the axis, and ∫₀⁴ (x−2) = 0 because the area below on [0, 2] cancels the area above on [2, 4].
feedback.incorrect: Use (b² − a²)/2 for ∫ₐᵇ x, and remember area below the axis counts as negative. ∫₀⁴ (x−2) cancels to 0.
feedback.hint: Use (b² − a²)/2 for ∫ₐᵇ x, and count any strip below the axis as negative — then work out each value.

content.body: The curve f(x) = x − 1 spends part of this interval below the x-axis. Tap the stretch where it contributes NEGATIVE area to the integral.

answer.prompt: Tap the stretch where the curve dips below the axis.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on this different line and watch the signed area drop wherever the curve dips below the axis.

feedback.correct: Right — on [0, 1] the curve sits below the axis, so those strips count as negative and pull the integral down.
feedback.incorrect: Negative area comes from where the curve is below the x-axis. Look for where f(x) = x − 1 is less than 0.
feedback.hint: The strip is negative only where the curve sits below the axis — locate where x − 1 < 0 and tap there.

content.body: The shaded region under f(x) = x² runs from 0 to 2. Without computing the numbers, which half holds more area: the slice from x = 0 to 1, or the slice from x = 1 to 2?

options: The slice from 1 to 2 — the curve is much taller there, so its strips enclose more area
The slice from 0 to 1 — area always piles up fastest near the start
They hold equal area, since the two slices are the same width
There's no way to tell without computing both integrals

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit under this different curve and watch the area pile up faster where the curve is taller.

feedback.correct: Right — both slices are 1 unit wide, but x² is far taller on [1, 2], so its strips enclose much more area (about 2.3 vs 0.3).
feedback.incorrect: Both slices have the same width, so the taller one wins. Compare how high the curve sits on each half.
feedback.hint: Both slices are the same width, so the taller one holds more area — compare how high x² rises on each half.

content.body: For the flat line f(x) = 2, drag t until the shaded area from 1 to t equals 6.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit under this different flat line and watch the rectangle's area accumulate.

feedback.correct: Right — 2(t − 1) = 6 gives t − 1 = 3, so t = 4. The width runs from 1 to 4, not from 0.
feedback.incorrect: The area is height × width = 2(t − 1). Set 2(t − 1) = 6 and solve for t.
feedback.hint: The area is height × width = 2(t − 1) — set 2(t − 1) = 6 and solve for t.

content.body: Order these integrals from smallest area to largest.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on this different line and watch the area between them grow and shrink.

feedback.correct: Right — the areas are 1.5, 2, 4, and 8. Use (b² − a²)/2 for each.
feedback.incorrect: Compute each with ∫ₐᵇ x = (b² − a²)/2, then order them smallest to largest.
feedback.hint: Evaluate each with ∫ₐᵇ x = (b² − a²)/2, then line them up from smallest to largest.

content.body: The area under f(x) = x from 0 to b is a triangle. Drag the matching expression for that area into the blank.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the shaded triangle's area grow under this different line.

feedback.correct: Exactly — a triangle of base b and height b has area ½b².
feedback.incorrect: The region is a triangle: ½ · base · height, with base and height both b.
feedback.hint: It's a triangle with base b and height b — apply ½ · base · height, then pick the matching tile.

content.body: Here the curve f(x) = x − 2 dips below the axis, so area there counts as negative. The shaded region runs from 0 to t. Drag t to where the running (signed) area is the MOST NEGATIVE.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on this different line and watch the signed area keep subtracting while the curve is below the axis.

feedback.correct: Right — below the axis the area keeps subtracting, so the running total bottoms out exactly where the curve crosses zero, at t = 2 (area = −2).
feedback.incorrect: While f is below the axis the running area keeps dropping; once f turns positive it climbs back. The minimum is where the curve crosses the axis.
feedback.hint: While x − 2 sits below the axis the total falls, then it rises once the curve turns positive — so it bottoms out right where the curve crosses zero.

content.body: Set up the definite integral of f(x) = x that measures the area from x = 1 to x = 3. Drag the two handles to place the lower and upper limits.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on this different line to set a definite integral and watch the shaded area between them.

feedback.correct: Exactly — the shaded trapezoid runs from x = 1 to x = 3, the limits of the integral.
feedback.incorrect: Place the left handle at x = 1 and the right handle at x = 3 so the shaded region matches the interval.
feedback.hint: Read the interval straight from the prompt and drag each handle to that endpoint.

content.body: Position is the accumulation of velocity. Drag up or down on the plot to set your starting velocity, then press Start. After the 3-2-1 countdown the playhead sweeps left to right — keep your velocity steady so your accumulated position (the solid line) tracks the dashed target, position = t.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit under this different flat line and watch the area pile up as a steady running total — accumulation in action.

feedback.correct: Nice — a steady velocity of 1 accumulates into position = t, a straight line. That's integration in action.
feedback.incorrect: Set your velocity near 1 before you start, then hold it steady so the area you accumulate climbs alongside the dashed line.
feedback.hint: Position is the running total of velocity, so a steady velocity traces a straight position line — pick the constant speed that makes it climb to match position = t.
